content:
Goal
* Would you mind changing "smuggler" to something that doesn't imply illegal activity? (For example, first trader on the moon. You can use another idea.)

* Second sentence: Please remove "maximum".

Input
* Line 1: Please remove "maximum".

Output
* Please format "-1" as a {{constant}}.

Stub generator
* Please change all instances "long" to "int".

Solution
* Line 4 is not needed on this platform and can be removed.

Cases
* Case names: Would you mind changing "smuggling" to something that does not imply illegal activity?

* Please add several additional pairs of cases to ensure adequate coverage of different scenarios:

  • Exact [[capacity]] using all items. Use truly random [[weight]] and [[value]] between 10 and 30. [[itemCount]] between 6 and 8.

  • All items exceed [[capacity]]. Use truly random [[weight]] and [[value]] between 10 and 30. [[itemCount]] between 9 and 11.

  • A single item matches [[capacity]] and has the highest value. Use truly random [[weight]] and [[value]] between 30 and 100. [[itemCount]] between 12 and 14.

  • A single item matches [[capacity]], but multiple items together yield a higher value while also matching [[capacity]]. Use truly random [[weight]] and [[value]] between 100 and 1000. [[itemCount]] between 15 and 20.

  • Many different combinations match [[capacity]]. Include several groups of items, with all items in each group sharing the same [[weight]] but different [[value]]. The highest-value combination should include some items from at least two such groups. Use truly random [[weight]] and [[value]] between 1000 and 10000. [[itemCount]] between 20 and 30.

* Remember to order the cases appropriately when adding the new cases.

Thanks.

Some preliminary comments from me:

- This is a good introductory problem to dynamic programming and it is quite unique in terms of having the constraint being "exactly equal" instead of "at most", which limits the problem somewhat.

- Currently, the constraints specified are not reflected accurately in the test cases. The maximum capacity (which are all currently <50) is way too small to require comparing multiple subsets of items against one another, and the whole puzzle is currently brute-forceable. As it stands, the puzzle does not warrant the "Hard" difficulty. Please generate larger test cases.

- In terms of formatting, I would format -1 as a constant. The last sentence of the goal could also be stated clearer; "If it is not possible for the spaceship to carry items weighing exactly [[capacity]], output {{-1}} instead."

Hi everyone,
I’ve just published this puzzle as a Work In Progress and would really appreciate your feedback.
The goal was to create a simple but non-trivial optimization problem around an exact-weight knapsack variant, while keeping the statement clear and accessible.
I’m especially interested in your thoughts on:
- the clarity of the statement
- the difficulty level (currently set to “Hard”)
- the quality and progression of the test cases
- any potential edge cases I might have missed
If something feels unclear, too easy, too hard, or just “off”, feel free to point it out.
Thanks a lot for your time and help !

Thanks a lot for your detailed feedback.
I've updated the puzzle accordingly:

- The larger test cases have been reworked with significantly higher capacities and more items, so brute-force approaches are no longer viable and the difficulty better matches the intended "Hard" level.

- I also adjusted the constraints to better reflect the actual datasets used in the tests.

- The statement has been clarified, and {{-1}} is now formatted as a constant as suggested.

While working on the larger cases, I also improved the reference solution to ensure it handles them efficiently.

Thanks again for the helpful remarks!

To clarify, by "truly random", I mean the following:
* Use Python's `random` library (or an equivalent tool) to generate the numbers before making any necessary manual adjustments.
* Shuffle the items into a random order as well (this can also be done using the `random` library).

Hi, thank you very much for your detailed feedback!

I've applied all the requested changes:

- updated the theme to remove any reference to illegal activity,
- removed "maximum" from the statement and input,
- clarified the goal and formatted {{-1}} as a constant,
- updated the stub to use int,
- cleaned up and optimized the reference solution,
- adjusted the constraints to match the datasets,
- renamed all cases accordingly.

I also added new test/validator pairs to improve coverage (exact fill, no solution, single-item optimal, combination vs single item, multiple combinations, large solvable and unsolvable cases).
All datasets were generated using random and shuffled, with minimal adjustments.

Finally, I reworked and reordered the cases to better reflect the intended "Hard" difficulty.

Thanks again, your feedback was very helpful!

Thank you as well! You've done an excellent job addressing the earlier comments. Just one minor issue to fix:

Cases
* Deep Space Logistics (Test 9) and Deep Space Routing (Validator 9): [[capacity]] is 1000003, which exceeds the upper bound of 10^6 stated in the Constraints section. Without changing the constraint, please adjust [[capacity]] to exactly 10^6 and modify the weights if necessary.

Thanks again.

Thanks for spotting this.
I updated both Deep Space Logistics and Deep Space Routing so that [[capacity]] is now exactly 1000000, consistent with the stated constraints.
The datasets remain valid with that capacity, and I recomputed the expected outputs accordingly.
